does: This paper proposes GRADE, a method for aligning large language models that replaces policy gradient algorithms like PPO with direct backpropagation through a differentiable relaxation of the discrete token sampling process. The approach uses Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization combined with straight-through estimation (GRADE-STE) to enable end-to-end gradient flow from reward signals through generated tokens to model parameters. On sentiment-controlled text generation with the IMDB dataset, GRADE-STE achieves substantially better alignment performance (reward 0.763) compared to PPO (0.510) and REINFORCE (0.617), representing a 50% relative improvement over PPO. The method achieves this while reducing gradient variance by over 14 times compared to REINFORCE and maintaining stable training throughout.
connects: This work builds on the established RLHF paradigm for LLM alignment, which has become standard practice following successes with models like InstructGPT. GRADE responds directly to known limitations of policy gradient methods like PPO, which suffer from high variance in gradient estimates, requiring extensive computational resources and careful hyperparameter tuning. The core technical contribution leverages the Gumbel-Softmax reparameterization trick, a technique from the differentiable sampling literature developed for enabling backpropagation through discrete choices. The straight-through estimator component extends prior work on gradient estimation through non-differentiable operations. This approach bridges ideas from variational inference and discrete optimization with modern LLM training, addressing a key pain point in current RLHF implementations.
enables: GRADE points toward more efficient and stable methods for aligning large language models, potentially making high-quality alignment more accessible by reducing computational overhead and hyperparameter sensitivity. The demonstrated reduction in gradient variance could enable more reliable scaling of alignment to larger models and more complex reward functions. The approach opens directions for further investigation into differentiable relaxations of discrete sampling in language generation, potentially extending to other structured prediction tasks requiring alignment. The method could also facilitate tighter integration of reward signals during training and enable new forms of constrained generation where gradient information flows more directly. Success at scale could reshape the practical paradigm for LLM alignment away from policy gradient methods toward backpropagation-based approaches, with implications for how alignment objectives are formulated and optimized.
